# Kano Model - Cite-Only Pointer

This document is a cite-only pointer. It names the Kano model and the categorization structure the BRD Builder workflow references, links to a stable overview and the original academic citation, and does not redistribute upstream prose.

## What the Kano Model Is

The Kano model is a customer-satisfaction theory of product attributes published by Noriaki Kano and colleagues in 1984. It categorizes product attributes by the relationship between their presence and customer satisfaction. The five categories the BRD Builder references by name are:

* Must-be (basic): expected attributes whose absence causes dissatisfaction; presence does not produce satisfaction.
* Performance (one-dimensional): attributes whose presence produces satisfaction proportional to how well they are delivered.
* Attractive (delighter): unexpected attributes whose presence produces high satisfaction; absence does not cause dissatisfaction.
* Indifferent: attributes that produce no meaningful change in satisfaction either way.
* Reverse: attributes whose presence causes dissatisfaction for some customers.

Categorization is performed by asking customers paired functional and dysfunctional questions about each attribute and tallying responses against the Kano evaluation table. Categories shift over time as expectations evolve (today's attractive attribute can become tomorrow's must-be), so the upstream technique calls for periodic re-validation.

The BRD Builder references Kano by name as an alternative prioritization scheme for product-led BRDs where customer-satisfaction evidence is available or can be gathered. The BRD Builder does not redistribute Kano's original paper text or the published evaluation table prose.

## Why Cite-Only

The Kano model is described in academic publications and many secondary sources, each under its own terms. The original paper (Kano, Seraku, Takahashi, and Tsuji, 1984) and the canonical evaluation-table presentations are copyrighted by their respective publishers. The five category names are widely used industry terminology that can be referenced by name.

The BRD Builder's posture is:

* Reference the Kano model by name when stakeholders propose it for a product-led, customer-facing BRD.
* Use the five category names (must-be, performance, attractive, indifferent, reverse) in BRD templates without quoting upstream prose definitions or reproducing the evaluation table.
* Attribute the technique to Noriaki Kano whenever it is referenced in BRD outputs.
* Link to the upstream overview and academic citation when a stakeholder wants the canonical description.

## Upstream Sources

* [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kano_model](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kano_model) (accessed 2026-05-25) - stable overview describing the Kano model, its five categories, and the functional/dysfunctional questionnaire approach.
* Kano, N., Seraku, N., Takahashi, F., and Tsuji, S. (1984). "Attractive Quality and Must-Be Quality." *Journal of the Japanese Society for Quality Control*, 14(2), 39-48. - Original academic publication establishing the model.

## How the BRD Builder Uses Kano

The BRD Builder applies the Kano model under the following operating rules, which are original Microsoft synthesis informed by the scheme selector in [../SKILL.md](../SKILL.md):

* Kano is proposed only when the BRD covers a customer-facing product or experience and customer-satisfaction evidence is available or can be gathered before Define-exit.
* Every BRD prioritization section that declares Kano must record the evidence source per categorization (survey responses, interview quotes, observational data) so the categorization is auditable.
* Categories are re-validated at an interval agreed with stakeholders; the assessor flags Kano sections older than the agreed interval for refresh.
* The reverse category must be considered explicitly; omitting it is flagged as a pitfall because reverse attributes are easy to miss without direct customer evidence.
* Kano is not used for internal-tool BRDs where the customer-satisfaction axis is not meaningful; MoSCoW or RICE is preferred in those contexts.
